extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
36-year-old Japanese-American woman, architectural project manager. Lives in Denver, CO with her husband Ryan Cooper (age 38, high school English teacher). They have been trying to conceive for 2 years. Insurance: employer plan (UnitedHealthcare). Lisa is otherwise healthy — exercises regularly (yoga, hiking), non-smoker, occasional social drinker (stopped when trying to conceive).

## Clinical Scenario
Lisa has experienced three early pregnancy losses over 18 months:
- **Loss 1** (June 2024): Spontaneous abortion at 6 weeks, managed expectantly. Her OB/GYN attributed it to the statistical norm (15-20% of recognized pregnancies end in miscarriage) and recommended trying again.
- **Loss 2** (November 2024): Spontaneous abortion at 7 weeks. Her OB/GYN initiated a basic recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) workup: karyotyping of both partners (normal), TSH (normal), HbA1c (normal), and basic coagulation panel (normal).
- **Loss 3** (March 2025): Spontaneous abortion at 8 weeks, following a pregnancy that initially showed a heartbeat on 6-week ultrasound but had absent cardiac activity on 8-week follow-up. Products of conception sent for genetic testing — normal female karyotype (46,XX), ruling out embryonic chromosomal abnormality.

After the third loss, her OB/GYN suspects antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) based on the pattern of recurrent early losses with a chromosomally normal conceptus. She orders anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, and anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies. Anticardiolipin IgG is elevated (48 GPL, positive >40) and lupus anticoagulant is weakly positive. These need to be confirmed on repeat testing 12 weeks apart per revised Sapporo criteria.

Her OB/GYN refers Lisa to:
1. **Maternal-fetal medicine (MFM)** for high-risk pregnancy planning and management
2. **Hematology** for APS evaluation and anticoagulation planning for future pregnancies

Both specialists need access to Lisa's complete OB/GYN records — the pregnancy loss history, prior workup results, and APS lab results — to provide consultation.

## Sites
- **Rocky Mountain Women's Health** — OB/GYN in Denver, CO (2022–present). Routine GYN care, pregnancy management, RPL workup, APS screening.
- **Colorado Maternal-Fetal Medicine** — MFM specialist in Denver, CO (2025–present). High-risk pregnancy consultation, APS management planning.
- **UC Health Hematology** — Hematologist in Aurora, CO (2025–present). APS diagnostic confirmation, anticoagulation strategy.

Three sites, one state (CO). Tests Permission Ticket constraints on **scopes** (specialists need OB/GYN-specific data: Condition, Observation [labs, ultrasound findings], DiagnosticReport, Procedure [prior pregnancy management]) and **organizations** (OB/GYN → MFM + hematology referral chain).

note.text: Formally confirmed at follow-up encounter on 2025-07-23. Meets revised Sapporo (Sydney) classification criteria: clinical criterion (three consecutive unexplained early pregnancy losses before 10 weeks gestation) combined with laboratory criteria (persistently positive anticardiolipin IgG at moderate titer 52 GPL, persistently positive lupus anticoagulant, now-positive anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG at 22 units confirmed at >12 weeks). Risk stratification: medium risk. Patient is not triple-positive at high titer, which is a favorable prognostic factor. Thrombophilia screening entirely negative — no concurrent inherited thrombophilia.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

note.text: Three consecutive early pregnancy losses: spontaneous abortion at 6 weeks (June 2024), missed abortion at 7 weeks (November 2024), and embryonic demise at 8 weeks (March 2025). Third loss had normal female karyotype (46,XX). APS is the leading suspected cause.

code.text: Complete thrombophilia panel (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, protein C, protein S, antithrombin)

note.text: Complete inherited thrombophilia screening to rule out concurrent inherited thrombophilia. Includes Factor V Leiden mutation analysis, prothrombin gene mutation (G20210A) analysis, protein C activity, protein S activity (free and total), and antithrombin activity. To be drawn on or after 2025-07-07.

basedOn.display: Repeat anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies (IgG and IgM)

resource.conclusion: Persistently elevated anticardiolipin IgG (52 GPL, positive >40) and now-positive anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG (22 units, positive >20). Anticardiolipin IgM and anti-B2GP1 IgM remain negative. Confirms persistent medium-titer anticardiolipin IgG positivity on two occasions >=12 weeks apart.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

basedOn.display: Repeat lupus anticoagulant (dRVVT with mixing study)

coding.display: Lupus anticoagulant [Interpretation] in Platelet poor plasma

resource.conclusion: Lupus anticoagulant positive on dRVVT with confirmatory mixing study. Screen-to-confirm ratio 1.35 (positive >1.20). More clearly positive than initial weakly positive result, confirming persistent lupus anticoagulant positivity.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

resource.conclusion: Protein C activity (98%), protein S free (72%), protein S total (88%), and antithrombin activity (104%) all within normal limits. No functional deficiency of natural anticoagulant proteins identified.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

resource.conclusion: Factor V Leiden mutation and prothrombin gene mutation G20210A both not detected (wild type). No inherited genetic thrombophilia identified.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

resource.conclusion: Confirmatory antiphospholipid antibody panel positive. Anticardiolipin IgG persistently positive at moderate titer (52 GPL), lupus anticoagulant persistently positive, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG now positive (22 units). Antibody profile has strengthened on 12-week repeat testing. Meets revised Sapporo (Sydney) laboratory criteria for antiphospholipid syndrome.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

resource.conclusion: Thrombophilia screening entirely negative. No concurrent inherited thrombophilia identified. This is a favorable prognostic factor.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

reasonCode.text: Antiphospholipid syndrome, obstetric subtype — preconception thromboprophylaxis

dosageInstruction.text: Take 81 mg by mouth once daily, to begin preconception when patient is ready to attempt conception
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take one tablet (81 mg) by mouth once daily. Start when you are ready to begin trying to conceive.

security.display: sexuality and reproductive health information sensitivity

coding.display: enoxaparin sodium 40 MG in 0.4 ML Prefilled Syringe

reasonCode.text: Antiphospholipid syndrome, obstetric subtype — pregnancy anticoagulation prophylaxis

dosageInstruction.text: Inject 40 mg subcutaneously once daily, to initiate upon positive pregnancy test (prophylactic dose)
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Inject 40 mg under the skin once daily. Begin when you receive a positive pregnancy test. Your doctor will provide injection training.

note.text: To initiate upon positive pregnancy test. Monthly anti-Xa level monitoring (target peak 0.2-0.6 IU/mL). Dose adjustment if significant weight change. Transition to unfractionated heparin at 36 weeks. Hold 24h before planned delivery. Resume 6-12h postpartum (vaginal) or 12-24h (cesarean). Continue or transition to warfarin for 6 weeks postpartum.
